text: Jazz Guitar Licks teaches the core pedagogical principle of extracting transferable concepts from learned licks rather than parroting them wholesale, anchored in key terminology including blue notes (b3 and b5 from the blues scale), the phrygian dominant scale (fifth mode of harmonic minor) for V7 chords, the 3 to 9 arpeggio (dim7 from the 3rd of any 7th chord), and tritone substitution mechanics (playing a 7th chord a tritone away while preserving the 3rd and 7th). The chapter establishes essential bebop techniques such as triads for chord outlining, a balance between diatonic and chromatic lines, the enclosure technique (surrounding a target note for tension and release), and side-stepping (alternating between minor pentatonic scales a half step apart for modern jazz texture). Charlie Christian emerges as the master whose characteristic moves—enclosures, lower neighbor tones after leaps, and the b3 to 3 to 5 riff mixing blue notes with diatonic intervals—recur throughout, while additional vocabulary includes the major blues scale (major pentatonic plus b3), diminished scale construction (alternating whole and half steps), double stops over pedal tones, three-note-per-string legato adapted from rock, and Joe Pass's signature chord-melody rhythm technique of splitting bass from treble with selective accent.
